model_summary: Initial gVAR-ready observation model focused on six operationalized criteria of resting-related guilt and productivity beliefs (C01–C06) and five modifiable predictors (P01–P05) that proximate salutogenic shifts in rest, self-compassion, urge tolerance, sleep, and time management. Measurements rely on brief, six daily EMA prompts over a 14-day window to maximize within-person variance while avoiding redundancy; a one-time-daily diary anchors nocturnal states. The predictor-criterion set emphasizes nonredundant, actionable levers with plausible lag structures (0–6h, 0–24h) suitable for graphical VAR (gVAR) modeling. Measurement scales are primarily 0–9 Likert to enhance variance; passive data is reserved for future expansion to preserve feasibility and safety. The plan aligns with the complaint decomposition while incorporating HyDe and LLM-informed relevance signals to prioritize levers with greatest expected impact on the salutogenic mental state.
variable_selection_notes: Selected six criteria (C01–C06) directly reflecting guilt, beliefs about productivity, time pressure, intrusive self-talk, action urges, and restoration avoidance. To minimize redundancy and collinearity, five predictors (P01–P05) were chosen to cover rest structuring, self-compassion/mindfulness, urge tolerance, sleep hygiene, and time-blocking. This yields a compact, estimable design with strong within-person variance and interpretable lag structure. All variables are operationalized for momentary EMA; predictors are intentionally actionable levers with safe, well-established behavioral targets. Potential biases include self-report bias and day-to-day variability; plan includes centering/scaling and planned missingness strategies to mitigate these. ” ,{

momentary_ema.notes: Six prompts per day to capture fluctuations in criteria and predictors; blocks spread across wake periods with jitter to reduce anticipation bias.

daily_diary.notes: Optional nightly diary to anchor sleep timing and pre-sleep mood.

passive_streams.notes: No passive data collection in this initial model to minimize burden.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Initial variable set is designed for standard multilevel modeling and gVAR estimation; aims for stable lag estimation with 5 predictors x 6 criteria.
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Utilizes 1–9 Likert scales for most constructs to maximize within-person variance; plan includes centering (z-scoring) during analyses.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Prompt reminders and flexible reminder timing to mitigate missingness; multiple imputation or ML-based handling planned if missingness is non-ignorable.

safety_notes: Not a diagnosis. No dosing or pharmacological guidance. If acute risk emerges, advise clinical evaluation per standard care. This model is for data collection only and aims to support salutogenic state optimization within ethical guidelines.

measurement.notes: Captures current guilt intensity during rest periods.

predictor_variables.label: Structured Rest Blocks with Restorative Cueing
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Deliberate rest periods reduce guilt when resting and reframe rest as productive for restoration.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Scheduled 5–10 min breaks; short mindful breathing; light stretching.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today I completed a planned rest block; to what extent did you engage in that rest block (0-9)?
measurement.notes: Assesses adherence to planned rest blocks.

expected_effects.rationale: Rest adherence reduces guilt during rest.
Rest blocks may soften beliefs about wasted time.
Structured rest can reduce perceived time scarcity.
Rest blocks may decrease intrusive self-criticism during leisure.
Urge to act may attenuate as rest becomes structured.
Rest structure supports restorative activity engagement.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility with short, scheduled prompts.

source_variable.label: Belief that non-productivity equals wasting time

measurement.item_or_signal: Holds a rule/belief that time not spent on productive tasks is wasted or a negative use of time.
measurement.notes: Assesses belief strength during daily rest/productive boundaries.

criteria.label: Sense of falling behind/time scarcity
criteria.query_text_used: Sense of falling behind/time scarcity | Operational item/signal: Perceives that not using time productively causes being behind schedule/peers or losing progress toward goals. | Polarity: unknown | Notes: Captures perceived time pressure and progress concerns.

mapping.rationale: Variable C03 labels Sense of falling behind/time scarcity. The EMA item describes perceived time pressure from not using time productively and progress concerns (being behind schedule/peers). Leaf 3459 maps to time management focusing on meeting deadlines and timely task completion, which directly captures the mechanism of perceived time scarcity affecting progress. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates for a momentary, 0–9 Likert EMA measure of time-pressure–related productivity concerns.

criteria.label: Intrusive self-critical thoughts during leisure
criteria.query_text_used: Intrusive self-critical thoughts during leisure | Operational item/signal: Experiences frequent intrusive self-criticism during weekends/leisure that interrupt rest. | Polarity: unknown | Notes: Assesses cognitive intrusion during leisure.

mapping.rationale: Variable label matches intrusive self-critical thoughts during leisure, measured momentarily via EMA with 0–9 scale. The closest leaf is 'self_critical_rumination' (self-critical rumination) under depressive personality disorder, which explicitly captures self-criticism rumination as intrusive thoughts during everyday contexts like leisure. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates for this content.

measurement.item_or_signal: Experiences frequent intrusive self-criticism during weekends/leisure that interrupt rest.
measurement.notes: Assesses cognitive intrusion during leisure.

criteria.label: Urge/drive to act rather than rest
criteria.query_text_used: Urge/drive to act rather than rest | Operational item/signal: Feels a strong urge or compulsion to engage in productive actions instead of downtime. | Polarity: unknown | Notes: Measures urge intensity and readiness-to-act during rest periods.

mapping.rationale: Best-matching operational leaf for C05: Urge/drive to act during rest. The measurement item captures urge intensity and readiness-to-act during downtime; the leaf 'preparedness for action' (idx 5950) encodes a mental readiness state for taking action, aligning with both the label (urge/drive to act) and the EMA signal (0–9 Likert). It is the most specific, nonclinical operational construct among candidates that directly corresponds to readiness to act rather than rest.

criteria.label: Difficulty engaging in restorative activities
criteria.query_text_used: Difficulty engaging in restorative activities | Operational item/signal: Avoids or shortens restorative activities due to guilt or intrusive thoughts. | Polarity: unknown | Notes: Captures restorative engagement during rest periods.

mapping.rationale: Variable C06 (Difficulty engaging in restorative activities) maps to engagement in rest/restorative processes. The item relies on momentary EMA with 0–9 scale and targets engagement with rest/recovery activities during rest periods. Among candidates, the leaf 'balancing activity and rest' (idx 4104) lies in the rest and recovery cluster and directly captures the core mechanism: difficulty balancing activity with restorative/restorative engagement. This provides the most specific operational construct for restorative engagement within the gVAR framework, aligning with the pseudoprofile emphasis on rest, restoration, and time management levers.

measurement.item_or_signal: Avoids or shortens restorative activities due to guilt or intrusive thoughts.
measurement.notes: Captures restorative engagement during rest periods.

predictors.label: Structured Rest Blocks with Restorative Cueing
predictors.query_text_used: Structured Rest Blocks with Restorative Cueing | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Deliberate rest periods reduce guilt when resting and reframe rest as productive for restoration. | Intervention examples: Scheduled 5–10 min breaks; short mindful breathing; light stretching. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today I completed a planned rest block; to what extent did you engage in that rest block (0-9)? | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] / brief_and_motivational_approaches / coaching_and_behavior_change_support / *time_and_energy_prioritization

mapping.rationale: Best match: P01 aims to structure rest blocks with restorative cueing; the leaf 3040 corresponds to recovery_design / restoration_and_self_care / recovery_rituals_planning, capturing planning/restoration rituals as a behavioral lever. It aligns with EMA self-report measurement of adherence to planned rest blocks (0–9) and the mechanism of deliberate rest reducing guilt and reframing rest as restoration. Among candidates, this leaf is the most specific operationalization of 'structured rest blocks' rather than general sleep or cognitive strategies.

source_variable.label: Structured Rest Blocks with Restorative Cueing
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Deliberate rest periods reduce guilt when resting and reframe rest as productive for restoration.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Scheduled 5–10 min breaks; short mindful breathing; light stretching.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today I completed a planned rest block; to what extent did you engage in that rest block (0-9)?
measurement.notes: Assesses adherence to planned rest blocks.

expected_effects.rationale: Rest adherence reduces guilt during rest.
Rest blocks may soften beliefs about wasted time.
Structured rest can reduce perceived time scarcity.
Rest blocks may decrease intrusive self-criticism during leisure.
Urge to act may attenuate as rest becomes structured.
Rest structure supports restorative activity engagement.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility with short, scheduled prompts.

predictors.label: Self-Compassion and Mindfulness for Leisure
predictors.query_text_used: Self-Compassion and Mindfulness for Leisure | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Enhances self-kindness during leisure, reducing self-criticism and guilt that arise in rest contexts. | Intervention examples: Brief compassion-focused self-talk, MBCT/MBSR-inspired practices. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | During the last leisure period, rate your level of self-compassion toward yourself (0-9). | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] / third_wave_and_contextual_therapies / mindfulness_based_interventions / mbct_mbsr_core_practices / *relating_differently_to_thoughts_practice

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is PSYCHO / third_wave_and_contextual_therapies / mindfulness_based_interventions / mbct_mbsr_core_practices / relating_differently_to_thoughts_practice. This directly corresponds to MBCT/MBSR core practices that cultivate a decentered, mindful relation to thoughts—a mechanism aligned with self-compassion and mindful leisure engagement. The variable P02 targets self-compassion and mindfulness during leisure, and the EMA item measures self-compassion during the last leisure period (0–9). Thus, this leaf provides the most specific operational construct to map the variable, consistent with the pseudoprofile’s emphasis on actionable levers and within-person variance.

source_variable.label: Self-Compassion and Mindfulness for Leisure
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Enhances self-kindness during leisure, reducing self-criticism and guilt that arise in rest contexts.

measurement.item_or_signal: During the last leisure period, rate your level of self-compassion toward yourself (0-9).
measurement.notes: Assesses compassionate response during leisure.

expected_effects.rationale: Increased self-compassion reduces guilt about rest.
Mindfulness reduces intrusive self-criticism during leisure.
Compassion practices may facilitate engagement in restorative activities.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility with short, targeted prompts.

predictors.label: Urge/Delay Tolerance Training
predictors.query_text_used: Urge/Delay Tolerance Training | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Builds tolerance to urges to act, reducing impulsive rest avoidance. | Intervention examples: Urge surfing, delay strategies, compassionate self-talk. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Right now, rate your urge to engage in productive action and your ability to delay acting (0-9). | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / exposure_and_inhibitory_learning / response_prevention_and_urge_work / urge_tolerance_and_surfing / *compassionate_self_talk_during_urge

mapping.rationale: Best match: variable P03 'Urge/Delay Tolerance Training' corresponds to leaf 'urge_tolerance_practice' (idx 4014) under the urge_tolerance_and_surfing branch. The measurement item 'Right now, rate your urge to engage in productive action and your ability to delay acting (0-9)' directly assesses urge strength and delay capacity, aligning with the intended mechanism of increasing urge tolerance to reduce impulsive rest/production-avoidant behavior. This is the most specific operational construct among candidates for this predictor, and fits the EMA self-report design (six prompts per day) described in the pseudoprofile.

source_variable.label: Urge/Delay Tolerance Training
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Builds tolerance to urges to act, reducing impulsive rest avoidance.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Urge surfing, delay strategies, compassionate self-talk.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now, rate your urge to engage in productive action and your ability to delay acting (0-9).
measurement.notes: Assesses urge strength and delay capacity in real-time.

expected_effects.rationale: Urge tolerance training lowers immediate drive to act during rest periods.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: No pharmacological guidance; behavioral strategies only.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility with standard EMA prompts.

predictors.label: Sleep Hygiene & Circadian Regulation
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep Hygiene & Circadian Regulation | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Improved sleep routines support restoration and reduce nocturnal cognitive arousal that feeds guilt. | Intervention examples: Regular bedtimes, dim-light exposure in evening, wind-down routine. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Last night’s sleep quality (0-9) and consistency of bedtime (0-9 on a 0-9 scale). | Prior ontology hint: [BIO] / Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration / Sleep_Routines_and_Practices

mapping.rationale: Selected leaf maps to BIO / sleep_circadian_and_restoration / sleep_routines_and_practices / presleep_downshift / low_stimulation_evening_activities. This is the most specific operational construct aligning Sleep Hygiene & Circadian Regulation with an actionable presleep-downshift strategy. Mechanism: improved evening routines with low stimulation reduce nocturnal cognitive arousal and support restoration/circadian alignment, which in turn can enhance sleep quality and bedtime regularity. Measurement alignment: Last night’s sleep quality (0-9) and consistency of bedtime (0-9) captured via EMA fit the presleep_downshift focus; six prompts per day enable responsive assessment of sleep onset conditions in a momentary EMA framework.

source_variable.label: Sleep Hygiene & Circadian Regulation
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Improved sleep routines support restoration and reduce nocturnal cognitive arousal that feeds guilt.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Regular bedtimes, dim-light exposure in evening, wind-down routine.

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night’s sleep quality (0-9) and consistency of bedtime (0-9 on a 0-9 scale).
measurement.notes: Captures sleep quality and consistency responsively.

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep reduces evening guilt about resting.
Stable circadian routine may soften beliefs about wasting time.
Improved sleep reduces perceived time scarcity.
Sleep quality supports lower intrusive thoughts in leisure.
Sleep supports restorative engagement.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Standard sleep assessment; no clinical intervention.

feasibility.notes: Sleep items feasible with short EMA questions.

predictors.label: Time-Blocking for Valued Actions
predictors.query_text_used: Time-Blocking for Valued Actions | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Structured time-blocking aligns behavior with values, reducing time-based guilt and enhancing task-focused rest. | Intervention examples: Daily schedule blocks for 60–90 min; buffer time; clear prioritized tasks. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Today, how well did you implement time-blocking for valued actions? (0-9) | Prior ontology hint: [PSYCHO] / change_process_and_therapeutic_phases / behavioral_integration / time_blocking_for_valued_actions

mapping.rationale: Best operational match: 'values_aligned_time_allocation_plan' leaf under PSYCHO/change_process_and_therapeutic_phases / readiness_and_engagement / wellbeing_optimization_entry_path. It directly corresponds to time-blocking for valued actions, i.e., scheduling time to act in ways aligned with personal values. The measurement item 'Today, how well did you implement time-blocking for valued actions? (0-9)' maps to execution of this plan. Mechanism: structured time-blocking aligns daily tasks with values, reducing time-based guilt and enhancing task-focused rest; aligns with P05 as a practical, actionable lever to shift rest and productivity.

source_variable.label: Time-Blocking for Valued Actions
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Structured time-blocking aligns behavior with values, reducing time-based guilt and enhancing task-focused rest.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Daily schedule blocks for 60–90 min; buffer time; clear prioritized tasks.

measurement.item_or_signal: Today, how well did you implement time-blocking for valued actions? (0-9)
measurement.notes: Assesses practical execution of time-blocking strategies.

expected_effects.rationale: Time-blocking should reduce perceived time scarcity.
Structured time use may lessen beliefs of wasted time.
Clear blocks can reduce urges to act impulsively.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: No safety concerns beyond standard EMA burden.

feasibility.notes: 6 prompts/day to capture daily scheduling practice.
